title: Tamil Inscription Data Repository
description: A curated dataset of significant Tamil inscriptions spanning from the early Sangam age to the medieval period, covering stone edicts, copper plates, cave inscriptions, and temple records.
sources: Tamil Nadu State Department of Archaeology

inscriptions.name: Pugalur Cave Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records donations by Chera kings. Mentions Cheral Irumporai and his family members. Regarded as one of the earliest Tamil inscriptions.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest Tamil-Brahmi inscriptions; confirms the historicity of Sangam-era Chera kings.

inscriptions.name: Mangulam Cave Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records a Jain ascetic's cave shelter donated by a local chieftain. One of the oldest Tamil-Brahmi records.
inscriptions.significance: Helps establish the earliest chronology of Tamil-Brahmi script; shows early Jainism in Tamil Nadu.

inscriptions.name: Edakal Cave Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Ancient petroglyphs with human and animal figures, some with early Tamil-Brahmi script. Includes a figure interpreted as Jain Tirthankara.
inscriptions.significance: Contains the oldest known pictographic inscriptions from South India; bridges prehistoric and early historic Tamil culture.

inscriptions.name: Brihadesvara Temple Inscription (Rajaraja I)
inscriptions.content_summary: Detailed records of gifts to the temple — land grants, gold, jewels, cattle, dancing girls (devadasi), singers, and priests. Records construction of the vimana (tower) and consecration rituals. Over 100 lines carved on the base of the outer walls.
inscriptions.significance: Most comprehensive Chola administrative and religious record; a UNESCO World Heritage Site inscription. Provides invaluable data on Chola economy, temple administration, and social structure.

inscriptions.name: Anbil Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants to a Brahmin village near Anbil. Contains detailed genealogy of the Chola kings. Provides important data on Chola administration, land revenue, and taxation.
inscriptions.significance: Key source for Chola genealogy and land administration practices.

inscriptions.name: Uttaramerur Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the constitution (kudavolai system) for the election of village assembly members (variyam). Details qualifications, disqualifications, ward divisions, term limits, and duties of elected members. Two inscriptions from 919 CE and 921 CE exist.
inscriptions.significance: Often cited as the world's earliest known democratic constitution for local self-governance. Demonstrates sophisticated Chola village administration.

inscriptions.name: Tiruvalangadu Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the military conquests of Rajendra Chola I, including his northern campaign to the Ganges and naval expeditions to Southeast Asia (Kadaram/Sri Vijaya). Contains Chola genealogy from the earliest times.
inscriptions.significance: Primary source for Rajendra Chola's naval empire and military campaigns. Documents India's connection with Southeast Asian kingdoms.

inscriptions.name: Kanyakumari Rock Inscription (Sthanu Ravi)
inscriptions.content_summary: Records military achievements and temple donations by the Ay king Sthanu Ravi. Mentions subjugation of surrounding chieftains and donation to a local deity.
inscriptions.significance: Important record of the Ay dynasty, a lesser-known but historically significant South Indian dynasty.

inscriptions.name: Velvikudi Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records restoration of a village (Velvikudi) to Brahmins after it was seized by Pallavas and subsequently recovered by the Pandya king. Contains detailed Pandya genealogy and mentions the Sangam-era Pandya king Nedunjeliyan.
inscriptions.significance: Provides continuity of Pandya dynasty from Sangam period to medieval times. Key source for understanding Pandya-Pallava conflict.

inscriptions.name: Pallava Granthavali – Mahendravarman Inscription (Mandagapattu)
inscriptions.content_summary: Claims credit for building the first cave temple in South India without the use of bricks, timber, metal, or mortar — carved entirely from living rock. Dedicated to Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva.
inscriptions.significance: First epigraphic record of rock-cut temple architecture in South India. Establishes Mahendravarman I as the pioneer of Dravidian rock-cut architecture.

inscriptions.name: Aihole Inscription (Pulakesi II)
inscriptions.content_summary: Praises Pulakesi II's victories over Pallava king Mahendravarman I. References the defeat of the Cholas, Keralas, and Pandyas. Composed by the court poet Ravikirti in Sanskrit.
inscriptions.significance: Key record for Chalukya-Pallava conflicts and the political landscape of 7th century South India, including references to Tamil kingdoms.

inscriptions.name: Kalugumalai Jain Inscriptions
inscriptions.content_summary: Records donations and names of Jain monks and laypeople who patronized the rock-cut Jain beds and sculptures at Kalugumalai. Contains a large unfinished rock-cut temple (Vettuvan Koil).
inscriptions.significance: Demonstrates the flourishing of Jainism in the Pandya kingdom. The unfinished temple is a rare archaeological record of ancient construction methods.

inscriptions.name: Thirunamanallur Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants and tax exemptions for the temple of Thirunamanallur Mahalingam. Details local administrative divisions (nadus) and measurement units used in Chola Tamil Nadu.
inscriptions.significance: Provides insights into Chola administrative geography and taxation system.

inscriptions.name: Sinnamanur Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Contains Pandya genealogy from early kings to Rajasimha II. Records village grants to Brahmins. Provides information on trade routes, local merchants, and village governance in the Pandya kingdom.
inscriptions.significance: Important record of the later Pandya dynasty and their administrative practices.

inscriptions.name: Mamallapuram (Mahabalipuram) Shore Temple Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records royal titles and religious dedications of Narasimhavarman II. Praises his achievements in war, administration, and temple construction. The Shore Temple is dedicated to Shiva and Vishnu.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest structural temples (not rock-cut) in South India; UNESCO World Heritage Site. Inscription establishes Pallava rule and artistic patronage.

inscriptions.name: Tanjore Saraswati Mahal Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Administrative colophons and acquisition seals recording Tamil manuscripts collected by Serfoji II for the Saraswati Mahal Library. Records titles of medical, literary, and scientific manuscripts.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the preservation of Tamil literary heritage by the Maratha king Serfoji II, who collected over 30,000 manuscripts.

inscriptions.name: Thiruvalangadu Plates – Naval Expedition Reference
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the naval campaign of Rajendra Chola I against Srivijaya (modern-day Sumatra and Malaysia). Lists conquered territories including Kadaram, Pannai, Malaiyur, and Mapappalam. Confirms Chola maritime supremacy in Southeast Asia.
inscriptions.significance: Primary epigraphic evidence for the largest pre-modern naval campaign in Asian history; demonstrates Tamil/Chola mercantile and military reach across the Indian Ocean.

inscriptions.name: Vatapi (Badami) Cave Inscription of Pulakesi I
inscriptions.content_summary: Records cave temple construction by Chalukya king Mangalesa. References conflicts with and subjugation of Tamil and Kadamba chiefs. Provides early medieval context for inter-dynastic Tamil relations.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest dated Chalukya records; sheds light on the northern boundary of Tamil political influence in early medieval India.

inscriptions.name: Vaikunta Perumal Temple Inscription (Nandivarman II)
inscriptions.content_summary: Carved narrative panels and inscriptions record the life of Nandivarman II, including his birth from a distant Pallava line, wars with Rashtrakutas and Gangas, and temple construction at Kanchipuram.
inscriptions.significance: Unique visual + textual historical record. Among the earliest narrative sculptural histories in Tamil Nadu.

inscriptions.name: Velurpalaiyam Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records genealogy of Eastern Chalukya kings and grants made to Brahmin settlements. Provides cross-dynastic link between Chalukyas and Chola territory.
inscriptions.significance: Demonstrates the inter-dynastic relationships and territorial overlaps between Tamil and Telugu kingdoms in the 9th century.

inscriptions.name: Karandai Tamil Sangam Plates

location.site: Karandai (Original; now at Leiden Museum, Netherlands)

inscriptions.discovered_by: Dutch traders; later acquired by Leiden University
inscriptions.content_summary: One of the largest and most detailed Chola copper plate grants. Records the founding of a brahmadeya village called Rajendrasimha-chaturvedimangalam. Contains extensive Chola genealogy and administrative geography.
inscriptions.significance: Largest known Chola copper plate grant; now preserved in Leiden, Netherlands. Demonstrates the spread of Chola administrative documents to global archives.

inscriptions.name: Srivaikuntam Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records grants made to the Vaikuntanatha temple and administrative orders issued by the Pandya king. Details land measurement, irrigation rights, and temple management norms.
inscriptions.significance: Key record of Pandya temple administration in the 13th century and the rights of local peasant communities.

inscriptions.name: Pugalur Brahmi Inscription No. 2
inscriptions.content_summary: Records a gift of a cave shelter by Ilam Kadungo, a Chera prince. Mentions the name of a Brahmin recipient. Demonstrates royal patronage of religious communities in early Sangam era.
inscriptions.significance: Corroborates the existence of Sangam-era Chera royalty using Brahmi script; shows bilingual cultural milieu (Tamil + Brahmi).

inscriptions.name: Dalavaipuram Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records a land grant by the Pandya king to a Brahmin village. Contains poetic verses in Tamil praising the king's lineage. One of the earliest Tamil copper plates in pure Tamil (without Sanskrit admixture).
inscriptions.significance: Demonstrates high Tamil literary quality in administrative records; shows resistance to Sanskritization in Pandya inscriptions.

inscriptions.name: Thiruvorriyur Pallava Rock Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the military exploits of Pallava king Aparajitavarman against the Pandyas and Gangas. Mentions the defeat of the Chola-Pallava alliance. One of the last Pallava inscriptions before the dynasty's decline.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the closing chapter of Pallava rule and the shifting political balance toward the Cholas in the late 9th century.

inscriptions.name: Thiruvannamalai Vijayanagara Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants and gold donations made by Krishnadevaraya to the Arunachalesvara temple. Praises the king's military victories and religious patronage. Provides information on Vijayanagara-period temple economy.
inscriptions.significance: One of the few Vijayanagara inscriptions in Tamil Nadu documenting Krishnadevaraya's direct patronage of Tamil temples.

inscriptions.name: Madurai Nayak Rock Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records construction of the palace complex including the Swarga Vilasam (Celestial Hall) and Nataka Sabha. Lists craftsmen guilds from Gujarat, Rajputana, and local Tamil territories. Documents the Nayak administrative and revenue system.
inscriptions.significance: Primary record for Nayak-period architectural patronage and the multi-regional craft guilds that built the palace.

inscriptions.name: Ramanathapuram Sethupathi Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records establishment of the Sethupathi kingdom after the decline of Nayak power. Documents grants to Brahmin communities and Hindu temples. Contains early references to Dutch and Portuguese traders operating in the region.
inscriptions.significance: Provides records of the transition from Nayak to Sethupathi rule and early colonial contacts with European traders.

inscriptions.name: Kanchipuram Ekambareshvara Temple Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records donations of gold, land, and cattle by the Vijayanagara governor to the Ekambareshvara temple. Details the gopuram (tower) construction and lists 500 devadasi women dedicated to temple service.
inscriptions.significance: Documents Vijayanagara temple expansion at Kanchipuram and the institutionalization of devadasi service in the medieval period.

inscriptions.name: Polonnaruwa Tamil Inscription (Sri Lanka)
inscriptions.content_summary: Tamil inscription recording the Chola administrative control of northern Sri Lanka. Documents tax collection, temple donations, and the appointment of Tamil governors. Mentions the city of Polonnaruwa as the Chola capital of the island.
inscriptions.significance: Key evidence of Chola administrative presence in Sri Lanka; demonstrates trans-oceanic reach of Tamil inscriptional tradition.

inscriptions.name: Tirunelveli Pandya Velvikudi Supplementary Grant
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the expansion of earlier Pandya land grants. Describes local revenue divisions, water rights for irrigation tanks, and temple maintenance endowments. Documents the village assembly's role in overseeing irrigation.
inscriptions.significance: Shows continuity of Pandya administrative practices and the critical role of irrigation management in South Indian temple economies.

inscriptions.name: Gangaikonda Cholapuram Inscriptions
inscriptions.content_summary: Records the construction of the Brihadeeswara temple at the new Chola capital Gangaikonda Cholapuram. Documents the Ganga Kondal water reservoir (Royal Tank) built to represent the Ganges brought from the north. Lists military trophies and gifts from subdued kings.
inscriptions.significance: Documents Rajendra Chola's symbolic and administrative achievements; the temple and tank are UNESCO-listed World Heritage Sites.

inscriptions.name: Tanjore Nayak Period Saraswati Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records renovations to the Brihadesvara temple under Nayak administration. Documents the addition of the Subrahmanya shrine and outer gopura. Lists musicians, dancers, and priests employed at the temple during the Nayak period.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the Nayak-period expansion of the great Chola temple and the continuation of its performing arts traditions.

inscriptions.name: Tirupati Balaji Temple Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Tamil-language record of donations by Tamil merchants and landowners to Tirumala Venkateswara. Records the appointment of Tamil Vaishnava priests (Thenkalai sect) and the institution of specific Tamil rituals. Documents income from Tamil traders' guilds.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the strong Tamil Vaishnava presence at Tirupati and the role of Tamil merchant guilds in temple patronage beyond Tamil Nadu.

inscriptions.name: Jambai Brahmi Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records a rock shelter donated to Jain ascetics by a local chieftain. Consists of three lines in Tamil-Brahmi noting the donor's name and lineage. One of the largest Tamil-Brahmi rock inscriptions.
inscriptions.significance: Provides evidence of Jain communities in early Tamil Nadu and adds to the corpus of Tamil-Brahmi texts from outside Madurai district.

inscriptions.name: Valuvur Hero Stones Group
inscriptions.content_summary: A cluster of 17 hero stones commemorating warriors who fell defending cattle raids and border conflicts. Each stone bears a carved warrior image, a horse or cattle motif, and a Tamil inscription naming the hero and his lineage. Some record women who performed sati after their husband's death.
inscriptions.significance: The largest known cluster of hero stones in a single site in Tamil Nadu; provides rich data on warrior sociology and cattle-raiding in early medieval Tamil culture.

inscriptions.name: Kulottunga Chola III Thanjavur Grant
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants and military victories of Kulottunga III. Mentions campaigns against the Pandyas, Hoysalas, and Eastern Chalukyas. Documents gifts to the Nataraja temple at Chidambaram and the Brihadesvara temple. Praises the king's poetic accomplishments.
inscriptions.significance: Documents the military and cultural achievements of one of the last great Chola kings, including notable battles with the Pandyas and Hoysalas.

inscriptions.name: Thiruvenkadu Copper Plates (Sundaresvara Grant)
inscriptions.content_summary: Records land grants to the Sundaresvara (Shiva) temple at Thiruvenkadu. Documents the village assembly's role in administering temple land. Contains references to trade with Sri Lanka and coastal merchants.
inscriptions.significance: Provides evidence of early Chola coastal trade networks and the administrative role of village assemblies in temple management.

inscriptions.name: Madurai Meenakshi Temple Pandya Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records renovation of the Meenakshi temple's golden lotus pond and the addition of a new mandapam. Lists donations by merchants and local guilds. Documents the establishment of a Tamil literary academy (Tamil sangam) within the temple complex.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest records of the Meenakshi temple's medieval expansion and provides evidence of a Tamil literary institution associated with the temple.

inscriptions.name: Karuvur (Karur) Satavahana Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Prakrit inscription found in the ancient Tamil city of Karuvur (modern Karur), recording a gift by a Satavahana merchant to a Buddhist monastery. Demonstrates trans-regional trade and cultural contacts between the Satavahana kingdom and Tamil Sangam territory.
inscriptions.significance: Evidences the presence of northern Indian merchants and Buddhist communities in the heart of Chera Sangam territory; shows multilingual commercial contacts.

inscriptions.name: Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar) Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Brick stamps and pottery inscriptions from the ancient Chola port city of Kaveripoompattinam (Puhar), the setting of the Tamil epic Cilappatikaram. Records merchants' names, trade goods, and port registration marks. Evidences Roman and Greek pottery also found at site.
inscriptions.significance: Material evidence confirming the existence of the Sangam-era port city; provides archaeological support for the Cilappatikaram narrative.

inscriptions.name: Velankanni Church Tamil Votive Inscription

location.site: Basilica of Our Lady of Good Health, Velankanni

inscriptions.content_summary: A Tamil-language votive inscription recording a miraculous healing and dedication to the local shrine, one of the earliest records of the Velankanni marian devotion. Demonstrates early Christian-Tamil cultural syncretism under Portuguese colonial influence.
inscriptions.significance: One of the oldest Tamil inscriptions recording Christian devotion; illustrates the adoption of Tamil epigraphic tradition by early Tamil Christians.

inscriptions.name: Thiruvarur Tyagaraja Temple Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records renovations to the Tyagarajaswamy temple chariot (ratham) and the conducting of the annual chariot festival (brahmotsavam). Lists musicians, drummers, and temple servants. Details measurement of the chariot's dimensions and the cost of renovation.
inscriptions.significance: One of the earliest records of the famous Thiruvarur temple chariot festival, considered the largest wooden chariot in South India.

inscriptions.name: Chidambaram Nataraja Temple Copper Plates
inscriptions.content_summary: Records large donations of gold, cattle, and land to the Nataraja temple by Virarajendra Chola. Documents the hereditary rights of the Dikshitar priest community and their responsibility for maintaining the cosmic dance icon. Establishes the unique Dikshitar administrative autonomy.
inscriptions.significance: One of the foundational records establishing Dikshitar autonomy at Chidambaram; Nataraja temple is a UNESCO-associated heritage monument.

inscriptions.name: Srirangam Ranganathaswamy Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records large donations by Vijayanagara vassals to the Ranganathaswamy temple including the addition of the Garuda mandapam and the outer two prakarams (enclosures). Lists Tamil Vaishnava scholars and their stipends. Documents the temple's role as the largest temple complex in India.
inscriptions.significance: Documents medieval expansion of Srirangam, the world's largest functioning Hindu temple complex, under Vijayanagara patronage.

inscriptions.name: Amaravati Buddhist Stupa Tamil Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Tamil-Brahmi inscriptions on the marble railings of the Amaravati Buddhist Stupa, recording donations by Tamil merchants and a Tamil king's envoy. Demonstrates that Tamil traders traveled as far as the Krishna River delta to donate to Buddhist monuments.
inscriptions.significance: Evidence of Tamil participation in Buddhist patronage outside Tamil Nadu; demonstrates trade connections between early Tamil kingdoms and the Krishna-Godavari region.

inscriptions.name: Anbil Plates (Sundara Chola — Second Set)
inscriptions.content_summary: A continuation of the first Anbil plates, recording additional land grants and clarifications of boundary markers. Contains a detailed description of village geography, irrigation canals, and tree-lined boundaries. Mentions measurement in local units (kuli, veli).
inscriptions.significance: Complements TI-005; together the two Anbil plates provide an exceptionally detailed picture of Chola land administration and village geography.

inscriptions.name: Thanjavur Maratha Tamil-Telugu Bilingual Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: A bilingual Tamil-Telugu inscription recording donations by the Maratha king Shahaji (father of Chhatrapati Shivaji's half-brother) to the Brihadesvara temple. Documents the Maratha adoption of local Tamil administrative and temple rituals. Lists Telugu-speaking courtiers alongside Tamil priests.
inscriptions.significance: Rare bilingual Tamil-Telugu record from the Maratha period; illustrates the multilingual character of the Thanjavur Maratha court.

inscriptions.name: Kumari Amman Cape Comorin Inscription
inscriptions.content_summary: Records renovation of the Kumari Amman shrine at the southernmost tip of India. Documents ceremonial bathing rituals at the confluence of three seas (triveni sangam). Lists pilgrims from distant kingdoms including Sri Lanka, Kerala, and the Chola-held eastern coast.
inscriptions.significance: One of the few medieval records from the iconic Cape Comorin site; documents its trans-regional pilgrimage significance.

inscriptions.name: Manimangalam Tamil Hero Stone
inscriptions.content_summary: A hero stone (nadukal/virukkal) commemorating a warrior who died defending cattle from a raid (cattle lifting — a common Sangam-era practice). Carved with a warrior figure and Tamil inscription detailing the hero's name and deed.
inscriptions.significance: Hero stones are a uniquely Tamil commemorative tradition; this is a well-preserved example showing warrior culture in early medieval Tamil Nadu.

inscription_types.description: Inscriptions carved on natural cave walls, often recording donations of cave shelters to Jain or Buddhist monks.
Inscriptions on the walls of Hindu temples recording grants, construction details, and royal achievements.
Grants issued on copper plates recording land donations, usually to Brahmins or temples.
Memorial stones erected in honor of warriors who died in battle, protecting cattle, or defending their community.
Inscriptions carved on exposed rock surfaces, often in open-air settings.
Inscriptions within temples carved from living rock.
Inscriptions on stone pillars, often royal proclamations.

scripts_glossary.description: Adaptation of Brahmi script to write Tamil. Used in the earliest Tamil inscriptions found in cave shelters.
A cursive Tamil script ('round script') used widely in early medieval Pandya and Chera records, especially in Kerala and southern Tamil Nadu.
A mixed script combining Grantha (for Sanskrit loan words) with Tamil letters. Widely used in Pallava and Chola inscriptions.
The script that evolved into the modern Tamil script, standardized from the 11th century CE during the Chola period.

key_themes: Tamil literary tradition in administrative records

metadata.note: Dates marked as BCE are negative values. CE dates are positive. All coordinates are approximate.
